title: The PMO & EPMO
summary: How the project management office and its enterprise form standardise delivery and link projects to organisational strategy.

sections.heading: What a PMO is and why organisations have one
sections.body:
A Project Management Office (PMO) is an organisational structure that standardises and supports project delivery and connects that delivery to wider strategy. Rather than every project inventing its own approach, the PMO provides a common home for methods, governance, reporting and shared capability.

Organisations create a PMO to raise consistency, improve oversight across many initiatives, build delivery capability and make sure projects actually advance the goals leadership cares about. The exam frames the PMO as an enabler of value, not just an administrative function.
sections.tip: PMO purpose = standardise delivery and link projects to strategy.

sections.heading: The three PMO types by degree of control
sections.body:
PMOs are classified by how much control they exert over projects.

- Supportive: consultative and low control. It supplies templates, training, best practices and lessons learned, and acts as a repository. Teams draw on it as needed.
- Controlling: moderate control. It requires compliance with a chosen framework, methodology, governance or set of tools, and checks that projects conform.
- Directive: high control. It takes over and directly manages the projects, with project managers drawn from and reporting into the PMO.
sections.tip: Supportive consults, Controlling enforces compliance, Directive runs the projects.

sections.heading: Core PMO functions
sections.body:
Across the three types, a PMO typically delivers a recognisable set of functions.

- Methodology and standards: defining the delivery approach, templates, processes and metrics.
- Training and coaching: building project management capability and mentoring practitioners.
- Delivery support and governance: tools, reporting, stage gates, assurance and oversight.
- Resource and multi-project management: sharing scarce resources and coordinating across the portfolio.
- Strategic alignment and benefits oversight: ensuring initiatives stay tied to objectives and that expected benefits are tracked.
sections.tip: Remember PMO functions as standards, capability, governance, portfolio coordination and benefits.

sections.heading: PMO vs EPMO
sections.body:
A PMO is usually focused on delivery efficiency. It improves how projects and programmes are run and typically reports into delivery or operational leadership.

An Enterprise PMO (EPMO) sits at the enterprise level. It links the C-suite's strategy to execution, owns strategic project selection and portfolio prioritisation, and reports to top executives. The EPMO answers the question of doing the right projects, while a delivery PMO concentrates on doing projects right.
sections.tip: EPMO is strategic and reports to the top; a PMO is delivery-focused and reports lower.

sections.heading: The project police anti-pattern and PMO value
sections.body:
A PMO that only audits and enforces, without helping teams succeed, becomes the project police. It creates friction, is resented, and is often the first thing cut because it is seen as overhead rather than value.

Effective PMOs enable and coach as well as govern, and they demonstrate measurable value such as faster delivery, fewer failed projects and tighter strategic alignment. PMO maturity grows from this basic, reactive support towards an optimised, strategic partner, and higher maturity correlates with better delivery outcomes.
sections.tip: Best answer: a PMO that enables and proves value, not one that just polices compliance.

source: PM Cram study notes — reworded for the current ECO-based PMP exam.
